question:
5. (i) Prove that if a function \( f \) is continuous on the closed interval \( [0, \pi] \) and if
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \cos \theta d \theta=\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin \theta d \theta=0
\]
then there exist points \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) such that
\[
0<\alpha<\beta<\pi \quad \text { and } f(\alpha)=f(\beta)=0
\]
(ii) Let \( R \) be any bounded convex open region in the Euclidean plane (that is, \( R \) is a connected open set contained in some circular disk, and the line segment joining any two points of \( R \) lies entirely in \( R \) ). Prove with the help of part (i) that the centroid (center of gravity) of \( R \) bisects at least three distinct chords of the boundary of \( R \).
solution:
Solution. (i) We may assume that \( f \not \equiv 0 \). Then since \( \int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin \theta d \theta=0 \) and \( \sin \theta>0 \) for \( 0<\theta<\pi, f \) must change sign somewhere on \( (0, \pi) \), say at \( \alpha \). If \( \alpha \) is the only zero of \( f \) on \( (0, \pi) \), then \( f \) has one sign on \( (0, \alpha) \) and the opposite sign on \( (\alpha, \pi) \). In the latter event,
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin (\theta-\alpha) d \theta \neq 0 .
\]

But
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin (\theta-\alpha) d \theta=\cos \alpha \int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin \theta d \theta-\sin \alpha \int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \cos \theta d \theta=0 .
\]

This contradiction implies the existence of a second point \( \beta \) with \( f(\beta)=0 \).
(ii) Take polar coordinates with pole at the centroid \( P \) of the bounded convex open region \( R \), and write the equation of the bounding curve \( \Gamma \) of \( R \) as \( \rho=g(\theta) \). [Since \( R \) is convex, each ray from \( P \) meets \( \Gamma \) just once. Thus \( \Gamma \) has such an equation.]
\( \Gamma \) is compact and the mapping \( (\rho, \theta) \rightarrow(1, \theta) \) (polar coordinates) of \( \Gamma \) into the unit circle is continuous and bijective; therefore, the inverse mapping \( (1, \theta) \rightarrow(g(\theta), \theta) \) is continuous. So \( g \) is continuous.

The moments of the region about the lines \( \theta=0 \) and \( \theta=\pi / 2 \) are given by
\[
\iint_{R} \rho \cos \theta \rho d \rho d \theta \text { and } \iint_{R} \rho \sin \theta \rho d \rho d \theta .
\]

These are both zero since we selected the origin at the centroid. Integration with respect to \( \rho \) gives
\[
\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[g(\theta)]^{3} \cos \theta d \theta=0=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[g(\theta)]^{3} \sin \theta d \theta .
\]

Since \( \cos (\theta+\pi)=-\cos \theta, \sin (\theta+\pi)=-\sin \theta \) this gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{0}^{\pi}[g(\theta)]^{3} \cos \theta d \theta-\int_{0}^{\pi}[g(\theta+\pi)]^{3} \cos \theta d \theta \\
& =\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[g(\theta)]^{3}-[g(\theta+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \cos \theta d \theta=0,
\end{aligned}
\]
and similarly
\[
0=\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[g(\theta)]^{3}-[g(\theta+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \sin \theta d \theta=0 .
\]

Now according to part (i),
\[
[g(\theta)]^{3}-[g(\theta+\pi)]^{3}=0
\]
holds for at least two values of \( \theta \) in \( (0, \pi) \). For these values
\[
g(\theta)=g(\theta+\pi),
\]
which means that the centroid bisects the chords having these two directions.
Now since we know that \( P \) must bisect at least one chord, we may as well assume that the polar axis was chosen in such a direction. Then the argument above shows that \( P \) also bisects at least two other chords.

descriptive_long.question:
5. (i) Prove that if a function \( funcval \) is continuous on the closed interval \( [0, \pi] \) and if
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \cos anglevar d anglevar=\int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \sin anglevar d anglevar=0
\]
then there exist points \( firstzero \) and \( secondzero \) such that
\[
0<firstzero<secondzero<\pi \quad \text { and } funcval(firstzero)=funcval(secondzero)=0
\]
(ii) Let \( convexrg \) be any bounded convex open region in the Euclidean plane (that is, \( convexrg \) is a connected open set contained in some circular disk, and the line segment joining any two points of \( convexrg \) lies entirely in \( convexrg \) ). Prove with the help of part (i) that the centroid (center of gravity) of \( convexrg \) bisects at least three distinct chords of the boundary of \( convexrg \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. (i) We may assume that \( funcval \not \equiv 0 \). Then since \( \int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \sin anglevar \, d anglevar=0 \) and \( \sin anglevar>0 \) for \( 0<anglevar<\pi, funcval \) must change sign somewhere on \( (0, \pi) \), say at \( firstzero \). If \( firstzero \) is the only zero of \( funcval \) on \( (0, \pi) \), then \( funcval \) has one sign on \( (0, firstzero) \) and the opposite sign on \( (firstzero, \pi) \). In the latter event,
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \sin (anglevar-firstzero) d anglevar \neq 0 .
\]

But
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \sin (anglevar-firstzero) d anglevar=\cos firstzero \int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \sin anglevar d anglevar-\sin firstzero \int_{0}^{\pi} funcval(anglevar) \cos anglevar d anglevar=0 .
\]

This contradiction implies the existence of a second point \( secondzero \) with \( funcval(secondzero)=0 \).
(ii) Take polar coordinates with pole at the centroid \( centroid \) of the bounded convex open region \( convexrg \), and write the equation of the bounding curve \( boundary \) of \( convexrg \) as \( radialvr=boundfun(anglevar) \). [Since \( convexrg \) is convex, each ray from \( centroid \) meets \( boundary \) just once. Thus \( boundary \) has such an equation.]
\( boundary \) is compact and the mapping \( (radialvr, anglevar) \rightarrow(1, anglevar) \) (polar coordinates) of \( boundary \) into the unit circle is continuous and bijective; therefore, the inverse mapping \( (1, anglevar) \rightarrow(boundfun(anglevar), anglevar) \) is continuous. So \( boundfun \) is continuous.

The moments of the region about the lines \( anglevar=0 \) and \( anglevar=\pi / 2 \) are given by
\[
\iint_{convexrg} radialvr \cos anglevar \, radialvr \, d radialvr \, d anglevar \text { and } \iint_{convexrg} radialvr \sin anglevar \, radialvr \, d radialvr \, d anglevar .
\]

These are both zero since we selected the origin at the centroid. Integration with respect to \( radialvr \) gives
\[
\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3} \cos anglevar d anglevar=0=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3} \sin anglevar d anglevar .
\]

Since \( \cos (anglevar+\pi)=-\cos anglevar, \sin (anglevar+\pi)=-\sin anglevar \) this gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{0}^{\pi}[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3} \cos anglevar d anglevar-\int_{0}^{\pi}[boundfun(anglevar+\pi)]^{3} \cos anglevar d anglevar \\
& =\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3}-[boundfun(anglevar+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \cos anglevar d anglevar=0,
\end{aligned}
\]
and similarly
\[
0=\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3}-[boundfun(anglevar+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \sin anglevar d anglevar=0 .
\]

Now according to part (i),
\[
[boundfun(anglevar)]^{3}-[boundfun(anglevar+\pi)]^{3}=0
\]
holds for at least two values of \( anglevar \) in \( (0, \pi) \). For these values
\[
boundfun(anglevar)=boundfun(anglevar+\pi),
\]
which means that the centroid bisects the chords having these two directions.
Now since we know that \( centroid \) must bisect at least one chord, we may as well assume that the polar axis was chosen in such a direction. Then the argument above shows that \( centroid \) also bisects at least two other chords.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. (i) Prove that if a function \( copperpipe \) is continuous on the closed interval \( [0, \pi] \) and if
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \cos cobblestone d cobblestone=\int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \sin cobblestone d cobblestone=0
\]
then there exist points \( butterleaf \) and \( sandcastle \) such that
\[
0<butterleaf<sandcastle<\pi \quad \text { and } copperpipe(butterleaf)=copperpipe(sandcastle)=0
\]
(ii) Let \( gardenplot \) be any bounded convex open region in the Euclidean plane (that is, \( gardenplot \) is a connected open set contained in some circular disk, and the line segment joining any two points of \( gardenplot \) lies entirely in \( gardenplot \) ). Prove with the help of part (i) that the centroid (center of gravity) of \( gardenplot \) bisects at least three distinct chords of the boundary of \( gardenplot \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. (i) We may assume that \( copperpipe \not \equiv 0 \). Then since \( \int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \sin cobblestone d cobblestone=0 \) and \( \sin cobblestone>0 \) for \( 0<cobblestone<\pi, copperpipe \) must change sign somewhere on \( (0, \pi) \), say at \( butterleaf \). If \( butterleaf \) is the only zero of \( copperpipe \) on \( (0, \pi) \), then \( copperpipe \) has one sign on \( (0, butterleaf) \) and the opposite sign on \( (butterleaf, \pi) \). In the latter event,
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \sin (cobblestone-butterleaf) d cobblestone \neq 0 .
\]

But
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \sin (cobblestone-butterleaf) d cobblestone=\cos butterleaf \int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \sin cobblestone d cobblestone-\sin butterleaf \int_{0}^{\pi} copperpipe(cobblestone) \cos cobblestone d cobblestone=0 .
\]

This contradiction implies the existence of a second point \( sandcastle \) with \( copperpipe(sandcastle)=0 \).
(ii) Take polar coordinates with pole at the centroid \( silkquartz \) of the bounded convex open region \( gardenplot \), and write the equation of the bounding curve \( mangrovese \) of \( gardenplot \) as \( brookfield=elmseedling(cobblestone) \). [Since \( gardenplot \) is convex, each ray from \( silkquartz \) meets \( mangrovese \) just once. Thus \( mangrovese \) has such an equation.]
\( mangrovese \) is compact and the mapping \( (brookfield, cobblestone) \rightarrow(1, cobblestone) \) (polar coordinates) of \( mangrovese \) into the unit circle is continuous and bijective; therefore, the inverse mapping \( (1, cobblestone) \rightarrow(elmseedling(cobblestone), cobblestone) \) is continuous. So \( elmseedling \) is continuous.

The moments of the region about the lines \( cobblestone=0 \) and \( cobblestone=\pi / 2 \) are given by
\[
\iint_{gardenplot} brookfield \cos cobblestone \, brookfield d brookfield d cobblestone \text { and } \iint_{gardenplot} brookfield \sin cobblestone \, brookfield d brookfield d cobblestone .
\]

These are both zero since we selected the origin at the centroid. Integration with respect to \( brookfield \) gives
\[
\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3} \cos cobblestone d cobblestone=0=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3} \sin cobblestone d cobblestone .
\]

Since \( \cos (cobblestone+\pi)=-\cos cobblestone, \sin (cobblestone+\pi)=-\sin cobblestone \) this gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{0}^{\pi}[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3} \cos cobblestone d cobblestone-\int_{0}^{\pi}[elmseedling(cobblestone+\pi)]^{3} \cos cobblestone d cobblestone \\
& =\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3}-[elmseedling(cobblestone+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \cos cobblestone d cobblestone=0,
\end{aligned}
\]
and similarly
\[
0=\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3}-[elmseedling(cobblestone+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \sin cobblestone d cobblestone=0 .
\]

Now according to part (i),
\[
[elmseedling(cobblestone)]^{3}-[elmseedling(cobblestone+\pi)]^{3}=0
\]
holds for at least two values of \( cobblestone \) in \( (0, \pi) \). For these values
\[
elmseedling(cobblestone)=elmseedling(cobblestone+\pi),
\]
which means that the centroid bisects the chords having these two directions.
Now since we know that \( silkquartz \) must bisect at least one chord, we may as well assume that the polar axis was chosen in such a direction. Then the argument above shows that \( silkquartz \) also bisects at least two other chords.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. (i) Prove that if a function \( constantval \) is continuous on the closed interval \( [0, \pi] \) and if
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \cos fixedangle d fixedangle=\int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \sin fixedangle d fixedangle=0
\]
then there exist points \( endingval \) and \( latevalue \) such that
\[
0<endingval<latevalue<\pi \quad \text { and } constantval(endingval)=constantval(latevalue)=0
\]
(ii) Let \( emptiness \) be any bounded convex open region in the Euclidean plane (that is, \( emptiness \) is a connected open set contained in some circular disk, and the line segment joining any two points of \( emptiness \) lies entirely in \( emptiness \) ). Prove with the help of part (i) that the centroid (center of gravity) of \( emptiness \) bisects at least three distinct chords of the boundary of \( emptiness \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. (i) We may assume that \( constantval \not \equiv 0 \). Then since \( \int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \sin fixedangle d fixedangle=0 \) and \( \sin fixedangle>0 \) for \( 0<fixedangle<\pi, constantval \) must change sign somewhere on \( (0, \pi) \), say at \( endingval \). If \( endingval \) is the only zero of \( constantval \) on \( (0, \pi) \), then \( constantval \) has one sign on \( (0, endingval) \) and the opposite sign on \( (endingval, \pi) \). In the latter event,
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \sin (fixedangle-endingval) d fixedangle \neq 0 .
\]

But
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \sin (fixedangle-endingval) d fixedangle=\cos endingval \int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \sin fixedangle d fixedangle-\sin endingval \int_{0}^{\pi} constantval(fixedangle) \cos fixedangle d fixedangle=0 .
\]

This contradiction implies the existence of a second point \( latevalue \) with \( constantval(latevalue)=0 \).
(ii) Take polar coordinates with pole at the centroid \( offcenter \) of the bounded convex open region \( emptiness \), and write the equation of the bounding curve \( linepiece \) of \( emptiness \) as \( diameter=flatfunc(fixedangle) \). [Since \( emptiness \) is convex, each ray from \( offcenter \) meets \( linepiece \) just once. Thus \( linepiece \) has such an equation.]
\( linepiece \) is compact and the mapping \( (diameter, fixedangle) \rightarrow(1, fixedangle) \) (polar coordinates) of \( linepiece \) into the unit circle is continuous and bijective; therefore, the inverse mapping \( (1, fixedangle) \rightarrow(flatfunc(fixedangle), fixedangle) \) is continuous. So \( flatfunc \) is continuous.

The moments of the region about the lines \( fixedangle=0 \) and \( fixedangle=\pi / 2 \) are given by
\[
\iint_{emptiness} diameter \cos fixedangle diameter d diameter d fixedangle \text { and } \iint_{emptiness} diameter \sin fixedangle diameter d diameter d fixedangle .
\]

These are both zero since we selected the origin at the centroid. Integration with respect to \( diameter \) gives
\[
\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3} \cos fixedangle d fixedangle=0=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3} \sin fixedangle d fixedangle .
\]

Since \( \cos (fixedangle+\pi)=-\cos fixedangle, \sin (fixedangle+\pi)=-\sin fixedangle \) this gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{0}^{\pi}[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3} \cos fixedangle d fixedangle-\int_{0}^{\pi}[flatfunc(fixedangle+\pi)]^{3} \cos fixedangle d fixedangle \\
& =\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3}-[flatfunc(fixedangle+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \cos fixedangle d fixedangle=0,
\end{aligned}
\]
and similarly
\[
0=\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3}-[flatfunc(fixedangle+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \sin fixedangle d fixedangle=0 .
\]

Now according to part (i),
\[
[flatfunc(fixedangle)]^{3}-[flatfunc(fixedangle+\pi)]^{3}=0
\]
 holds for at least two values of \( fixedangle \) in \( (0, \pi) \). For these values
\[
flatfunc(fixedangle)=flatfunc(fixedangle+\pi),
\]
which means that the centroid bisects the chords having these two directions.
Now since we know that \( offcenter \) must bisect at least one chord, we may as well assume that the polar axis was chosen in such a direction. Then the argument above shows that \( offcenter \) also bisects at least two other chords.

garbled_string.question:
5. (i) Prove that if a function \( qlmzxtrb \) is continuous on the closed interval \( [0, \pi] \) and if
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=\int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \sin qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0
\]
then there exist points \( hjgrksla \) and \( tnxwzrgh \) such that
\[
0<hjgrksla<tnxwzrgh<\pi \quad \text { and } qlmzxtrb(hjgrksla)=qlmzxtrb(tnxwzrgh)=0
\]
(ii) Let \( kdpsrjgh \) be any bounded convex open region in the Euclidean plane (that is, \( kdpsrjgh \) is a connected open set contained in some circular disk, and the line segment joining any two points of \( kdpsrjgh \) lies entirely in \( kdpsrjgh \) ). Prove with the help of part (i) that the centroid (center of gravity) of \( kdpsrjgh \) bisects at least three distinct chords of the boundary of \( kdpsrjgh \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. (i) We may assume that \( qlmzxtrb \not \equiv 0 \). Then since \( \int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \sin qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0 \) and \( \sin qzxwvtnp>0 \) for \( 0<qzxwvtnp<\pi, qlmzxtrb \) must change sign somewhere on \( (0, \pi) \), say at \( hjgrksla \). If \( hjgrksla \) is the only zero of \( qlmzxtrb \) on \( (0, \pi) \), then \( qlmzxtrb \) has one sign on \( (0, hjgrksla) \) and the opposite sign on \( (hjgrksla, \pi) \). In the latter event,
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \sin (qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) d qzxwvtnp \neq 0 .
\]

But
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \sin (qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) d qzxwvtnp=\cos hjgrksla \int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \sin qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp-\sin hjgrksla \int_{0}^{\pi} qlmzxtrb(qzxwvtnp) \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0 .
\]

This contradiction implies the existence of a second point \( tnxwzrgh \) with \( qlmzxtrb(tnxwzrgh)=0 \).
(ii) Take polar coordinates with pole at the centroid \( ztqfmxla \) of the bounded convex open region \( kdpsrjgh \), and write the equation of the bounding curve \( lvchqent \) of \( kdpsrjgh \) as \( vlsrhzpd=bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp) \). [Since \( kdpsrjgh \) is convex, each ray from \( ztqfmxla \) meets \( lvchqent \) just once. Thus \( lvchqent \) has such an equation.]
\( lvchqent \) is compact and the mapping \( (vlsrhzpd, qzxwvtnp) \rightarrow(1, qzxwvtnp) \) (polar coordinates) of \( lvchqent \) into the unit circle is continuous and bijective; therefore, the inverse mapping \( (1, qzxwvtnp) \rightarrow(bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp), qzxwvtnp) \) is continuous. So \( bxrmqozv \) is continuous.

The moments of the region about the lines \( qzxwvtnp=0 \) and \( qzxwvtnp=\pi / 2 \) are given by
\[
\iint_{kdpsrjgh} vlsrhzpd \cos qzxwvtnp vlsrhzpd d vlsrhzpd d qzxwvtnp \text { and } \iint_{kdpsrjgh} vlsrhzpd \sin qzxwvtnp vlsrhzpd d vlsrhzpd d qzxwvtnp .
\]

These are both zero since we selected the origin at the centroid. Integration with respect to \( vlsrhzpd \) gives
\[
\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2 \pi}[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} \sin qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp .
\]

Since \( \cos (qzxwvtnp+\pi)=-\cos qzxwvtnp, \sin (qzxwvtnp+\pi)=-\sin qzxwvtnp \) this gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{0}^{\pi}[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp-\int_{0}^{\pi}[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp+\pi)]^{3} \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp \\
& =\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3}-[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \cos qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0,
\end{aligned}
\]
and similarly
\[
0=\int_{0}^{\pi}\left\{[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3}-[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp+\pi)]^{3}\right\} \sin qzxwvtnp d qzxwvtnp=0 .
\]

Now according to part (i),
\[
[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)]^{3}-[bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp+\pi)]^{3}=0
\]
holds for at least two values of \( qzxwvtnp \) in \( (0, \pi) \). For these values
\[
bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp)=bxrmqozv(qzxwvtnp+\pi),
\]
which means that the centroid bisects the chords having these two directions.
Now since we know that \( ztqfmxla \) must bisect at least one chord, we may as well assume that the polar axis was chosen in such a direction. Then the argument above shows that \( ztqfmxla \) also bisects at least two other chords.

kernel_variant.question:
Let I:=[-\pi/6,5\pi/6] and define 
  u(\theta)=\sin(\theta+\pi/6),\qquad  v(\theta)=\cos(\theta+\pi/6).
A direct calculation shows that the two functions are orthogonal on I:
      \[\int_{I}u(\theta)\,v(\theta)\,d\theta =0.\]

i)  (Two-zero lemma with shifted weights)
Let \(f\) be a continuous function on I which satisfies
\[\int_{I}f(\theta)u(\theta)\,d\theta=0,\qquad\int_{I}f(\theta)v(\theta)\,d\theta=0 .\]
Prove that there exist two distinct points \(\alpha,\beta\in I\) such that \(f(\alpha)=f(\beta)=0\).

(ii)  (Bisected boundary chords for a uniform lamina)
Let R be a bounded open region in the plane that is strictly star-shaped with respect to a point P: every ray issuing from P meets \(\partial R\) in exactly one point and the distance \(\rho =|PQ|\) of this point from P depends continuously on the polar angle \(\theta\).  Equip the lamina occupying R with constant surface-density  \(\delta(Q)\equiv 1\).  Denote by \(P_{m}\) the centre of mass of the lamina.  Show that, if the reference point is chosen so that \(P=P_{m}\), then there are at least three different straight lines through P whose two boundary points are equidistant from P (equivalently: there are at least three distinct directions \(\theta\) for which \(\rho(\theta)=\rho(\theta+\pi)\)).  In other words, the centroid bisects at least three different boundary chords of the region.
kernel_variant.solution:
PART  (i)
Put
    \[\varphi:=\theta+\pi/6\;(0\le\varphi\le\pi),\qquad h(\varphi):=f(\varphi-\pi/6).\]
With this change of variable the two hypotheses become
\[\int_{0}^{\pi}h(\varphi)\sin \varphi\,d\varphi=\int_{0}^{\pi}h(\varphi)\cos \varphi\,d\varphi=0.\]
Because \(\sin\varphi>0\;(0<\varphi<\pi)\), the familiar two-zero lemma (Putnam 1982 B-5) yields two numbers
          \[0<\varphi_{1}<\varphi_{2}<\pi\quad\text{with }\;h(\varphi_{1})=h(\varphi_{2})=0.\]
Returning to the original variable gives the required zeros
          \[\alpha=\varphi_{1}-\tfrac{\pi}{6},\qquad \beta =\varphi_{2}-\tfrac{\pi}{6}\in I.\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
PART  (ii)  (uniform density)

Throughout we place the pole of the polar coordinate system at the centre of mass \(P\).  Let
          \[\rho=g(\theta)\qquad(0\le\theta<2\pi)\]
be the polar equation of the boundary.  Since R is strictly star-shaped, \(g\) is positive and continuous.

0.  Preliminaries.
For the uniform density the elementary area element is
    \[dA = \rho\,d\rho\,d\theta.\]
Integrating first with respect to \(\rho\) we obtain the linear mass element
    \[dM = \tfrac12 g(\theta)^{2}\,d\theta\]
and hence the total mass is \(M=\tfrac12\int_{0}^{2\pi}g(\theta)^{2}d\theta\).

Because the pole is the centre of mass, the first moments about the coordinate axes are zero:
    \[\int_{0}^{2\pi}\frac{g(\theta)^{3}}{3}\cos\theta\,d\theta=0,\qquad
      \int_{0}^{2\pi}\frac{g(\theta)^{3}}{3}\sin\theta\,d\theta=0.\tag{1}\]

1.  A first bisected chord.
For a direction \(\theta\) put
    \[A(\theta):=g(\theta)-g(\theta+\pi).\]
Because \(A(\theta+\pi)=-A(\theta)\) and \(A\) is continuous, there is a number
\[\theta_{0}\in(0,\pi)\quad\text{with }A(\theta_{0})=0,\]
that is, the chord through P in direction \(\theta_{0}\) is bisected by P.

Rotate the angular coordinate so that this direction becomes the new zero axis.  With the same symbol \(g\) we now have
\[g(0)=g(\pi).\tag{2}\]

2.  A function to which Part (i) applies.
Define
\[F(\theta):=g(\theta)^{3}-g(\theta+\pi)^{3}\qquad(0\le\theta\le\pi).\tag{3}\]
Then
\[F(0)=F(\pi)=0,\qquad F(\theta+\pi)=-F(\theta).\tag{4}\]

3.  Orthogonality relations for \(F\).
Using (3) and the change of variable \(\phi=\theta+\pi\) for the second integrals we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi} F(\theta)\cos\theta\,d\theta 
&= \int_{0}^{\pi} g(\theta)^{3}\cos\theta\,d\theta-\int_{0}^{\pi} g(\theta+\pi)^{3}\cos\theta\,d\theta\\[2mm]
&= \int_{0}^{\pi} g(\theta)^{3}\cos\theta\,d\theta+\int_{\pi}^{2\pi} g(\phi)^{3}\cos\phi\,d\phi\\[2mm]
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi} g(u)^{3}\cos u\,du = 0\quad\text{by (1).}
\end{aligned}
\]
Exactly the same computation with \(\sin\theta\) in place of \(\cos\theta\) gives
\[\int_{0}^{\pi} F(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta=0.\tag{5}\]
Thus \(F\) satisfies on the interval \([0,\pi]\) the hypotheses of Part (i).

4.  Two further bisected chords.
Applying Part (i) to the function \(F\) on \([0,\pi]\) we obtain two points
\[0<\alpha<\beta<\pi\quad\text{with }F(\alpha)=F(\beta)=0.\]
For these angles (3) gives \(g(\alpha)=g(\alpha+\pi)\) and \(g(\beta)=g(\beta+\pi)\); hence the chords through P in the directions \n\alpha and \beta are also bisected by P.

5.  Counting the chords.
Because of (2) we already had the bisected chord in direction 0.  Together with the directions \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) we have found at least three distinct directions through P along which the boundary points are equidistant from P.  Consequently the centroid bisects at least three different boundary chords of the region.

The theorem is proved.

core_steps: Orthogonality ∫f sinθ = ∫f cosθ = 0 forces f to change sign, giving at least one interior zero.
Assuming a single zero α, use ∫f sin(θ−α)=0 to contradict the sign pattern; hence a second zero β exists.
Place the origin at the centroid of R, write the boundary as ρ = g(θ); zero first moments ⇒ ∫[g³(θ)−g³(θ+π)] sinθ = ∫[g³(θ)−g³(θ+π)] cosθ = 0.
Apply the two-zero lemma to h(θ)=g³(θ)−g³(θ+π) to obtain two directions where g(θ)=g(θ+π).
Together with one obvious bisected chord, these two give a total of three centroid-bisected chords.

slot1.description: Angular interval of length π on which the weight sinθ is positive

slot2.description: Specific orthogonal weight functions used in the integral conditions

slot3.description: Odd exponent arising from the ρ-integration in the moment calculation

slot4.description: Geometric assumption ensuring each ray from the centroid meets the boundary exactly once
